title: Quantifying barriers of urban mobility
abstract: Barriers in cities, such as administrative boundaries, natural obstacles, railways or major roads are thought to induce segregation. However, the empirical knowledge about this phenomenon is limited. Here, we present a network science framework to assess barriers to urban mobility along their hierarchy, across residential areas and visited amenities. Using GPS mobility data, we construct a network of blocks from the sequence of individual stays in a major European city. A community detection algorithm allows us to partition this network into non-overlapping areas of dense mobility clusters, in which the effect of transportation hubs can be tuned with a parameter. We apply the Symmetric Area Difference index to quantify the overlap between these mobility clusters and the polygons of urban area separated by barriers. Reducing the effect of transportation hubs results in smaller scale mobility clusters that fit better to lower rank administrative or road barriers compared to their higher rank pairs. We find that characteristic urban barriers can replace each other in dividing mobility clusters of different scales. Next, we define the Barrier Crossing Ratio, the fraction of barrier crossings that link mobility clusters. An event study of bridge closure demonstrates that the Barrier Crossing Ratio can capture barrier effects even on small spatial scales. The decomposition of this indicator by origins and destinations suggests a significantly higher impact of barriers on those who live closer to the city center and smaller impact on visits to complex amenities. These results contribute to the ongoing discourse on urban segregation, emphasizing the importance of barriers to urban mobility in shaping interactions and mixing. • A network science framework is presented to quantify barrier effects in urban mobility. • Not just the physical barriers, but the administrative boundaries also limit urban mobility. • The hierarchy of urban barriers are reflected by the communities of the mobility network. • Urban barriers have significantly different effects by people's home location and the amenity complexity of destinations.
summary_en: Cities are full of barriers — administrative boundaries, rivers, railways, major roads — and those barriers are thought to shape social segregation. But by how much, and which barriers matter most? Using GPS mobility data for a major European city, this paper builds a network of urban blocks from individual stays and applies community-detection methods to identify where people actually mix. Lower-rank barriers (minor roads) align with small mobility clusters; higher-rank barriers (railways, major roads) align with larger ones. The paper provides a quantitative framework for prioritising which urban barriers to address to reduce segregation.
summary_hu: A városok tele vannak akadályokkal — adminisztratív határokkal, folyókkal, vasutakkal, nagy utakkal — és ezekről úgy gondoljuk, hogy alakítják a szociális szegregációt. De mennyivel, és melyik akadályok számítanak leginkább? Egy nagy európai város GPS-mobilitási adatait használva a tanulmány egy városi blokk-hálózatot épít az egyéni tartózkodásokból, és közösség-detektáló módszereket alkalmaz annak azonosítására, hol keverednek valójában az emberek. Az alacsonyabb rendű akadályok (kisebb utak) illeszkednek a kis mobilitási klaszterekhez; a magasabb rendűek (vasutak, nagy utak) a nagyobbakhoz. A tanulmány kvantitatív keretet ad annak prioritizálásához, mely városi akadályokat kell kezelni a szegregáció csökkentéséhez.
data_used: High-resolution GPS mobility data for a major European city (Budapest, 2010s–2020s); millions of individual-level stay sequences; matched to OpenStreetMap barrier data (roads, railways, rivers, administrative boundaries).
data_used_hu: Nagy felbontású GPS-mobilitási adat egy nagy európai városra (Budapest, 2010-es–2020-as évek); millió egyéni-szintű tartózkodási szekvencia; OpenStreetMap akadály-adatokhoz illesztve (utak, vasutak, folyók, adminisztratív határok).
policy_relevance: For Budapest Municipality urban planning: provides a method for ranking which urban barriers (specific road segments, railway crossings, administrative boundaries) most restrict social mixing. Useful for prioritising pedestrian bridges, crossings, and zoning changes. Directly applicable to Budapest; the method can be adapted for other Hungarian cities. Data is observational (GPS mobility); planning decisions still need causal analysis of specific interventions.
policy_relevance_hu: A Budapest Önkormányzat várostervezésének: módszert ad arra, hogy rangsoroljuk, mely városi akadályok (konkrét útszegmensek, vasúti átkelők, adminisztratív határok) akadályozzák leginkább a szociális keveredést. Hasznos gyalogos hidak, átkelők és zónázási változtatások prioritizálásához. Közvetlenül alkalmazható Budapestre; a módszer adaptálható más magyar városokra. Az adat megfigyelési (GPS-mobilitás); a tervezési döntések konkrét beavatkozások oksági elemzését igénylik.
